title: LLM Serving — Production Inference at Scale
description: LLM serving at scale requires orchestrating model deployment, continuous batching, request routing, autoscaling, and cost optimization. At Staff level, you must design serving infrastructure that handles 10M+ queries/day with <200ms latency and >95% availability.

sections.title: Serving Frameworks — vLLM, TGI, TensorRT-LLM, llama.cpp
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: vLLM (the industry standard) uses PagedAttention — OS-style virtual memory for KV cache that eliminates fragmentation. TGI (Hugging Face) pioneered token streaming and is the easiest to integrate with the HF ecosystem. TensorRT-LLM (NVIDIA) compiles model graphs with FP8/INT4 fused kernels for maximum throughput on H100/B200. llama.cpp targets CPU and edge with GGUF format and mixed quantization per layer.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: vLLM vs naive: 2-4× higher throughput via PagedAttention. TGI vs vLLM on Llama 3 70B (A100-80G): vLLM achieves ~30 tok/s vs TGI ~22 tok/s at batch size 64. TensorRT-LLM with FP8 on H100 achieves ~2× throughput vs FP16 on A100 for the same model. llama.cpp on M2 Ultra (192GB unified memory): runs 70B Q4_K_M at ~8 tok/s.

track_changes Decision Rule: Start with vLLM — it has the best community, most features (prefix caching, speculative decoding), and highest throughput. Use TGI if you need tight Hugging Face integration (transformers pipelines, PEFT adapters). Use TensorRT-LLM if you run on H100/B200 and need maximum throughput (cost-sensitive). Use llama.cpp for local/edge deployment (CPU, Mac, Raspberry Pi).

search Interview Angle: 'Compare vLLM and TGI.' vLLM uses PagedAttention (page-level KV cache management) and iteration-level scheduling (continuous batching). TGI uses a simpler static batching approach with selective batching. vLLM typically achieves 20-40% higher throughput on the same hardware.
sections.codeBlock:
# Framework throughput comparison (Llama 3 70B, A100-80G)
# Synthetic benchmark data — real results vary by workload

frameworks = [
    ('vLLM (continuous batch, max_batch=256)', 32, 8),  # tok/s, GB/seq
    ('TGI (simple batch, max_batch=64)', 22, 12),
    ('TRT-LLM FP8 (max_batch=128)', 50, 6),
    ('llama.cpp Q4_K_M (CPU, batch=1)', 8, 35),  # CPU tok/s, GB
]

for name, tps, mem in frameworks:
    throughput_per_gpu_1k = tps * 3600 / 1000  # K tokens/hr
    print(f'{name}: {tps} tok/s, {throughput_per_gpu_1k:.0f}K tok/hr, {mem}GB/seq')

# vLLM command (production)
# python -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server \
#   --model meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-70B \
#   --tensor-parallel-size 8 \
#   --max-num-seqs 256 \
#   --gpu-memory-utilization 0.90 \
#   --enable-prefix-caching

sections.title: Continuous Batching — Iteration-Level Scheduling
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Static batching waits for N requests to accumulate, processes them all to completion, then starts the next batch. If one sequence generates 50 tokens and another generates 500, the first finishes and waits idle. Continuous batching (used by vLLM) adds and removes sequences from the batch on-the-fly: after each transformer layer forward pass, completed sequences are ejected and new sequences are inserted. Every forward pass processes sequences at different positions in their generation — some at token 5, some at token 100, all in one batch.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Continuous batching delivers 5-20× throughput improvement over static batching at high request rates. For 100 concurrent requests with variable output lengths (32-2048 tokens): static batching achieves ~12 queries/sec, continuous batching achieves ~96 queries/sec. The improvement is larger when output lengths are more variable (long-tail distribution) because static batching wastes capacity on idle sequences.

track_changes Implementation: At each iteration: 1) Run forward pass for all active sequences. 2) Sample next token for each sequence. 3) If a sequence hits EOS or max_len, remove it from batch and return result. 4) If queue has pending requests and batch has capacity, add new sequence (pre-fill its prompt KV cache). 5) Pad remaining slots or use variable-length attention masks. vLLM uses a block manager to allocate KV cache pages to sequences dynamically.

search Staff Engineer Question: 'Our throughput plateaus at batch size 64. Increasing batch size to 128 doesn't help. Why?' You're hitting the memory wall — KV cache for 128 sequences at 32K context each exceeds available VRAM. The solution is not to increase batch size but to use PagedAttention (allocates KV pages lazily as sequences grow) or prefix caching (shares KV pages across sequences with identical prefixes).
sections.codeBlock:
# Continuous batching — conceptual scheduler
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import List

@dataclass
class Sequence:
    tokens: List[int]
    generated: int
    max_len: int
    finished: bool = False

class ContinuousBatchScheduler:
    def __init__(self, max_batch_size=64, max_seq_len=4096):
        self.active: List[Sequence] = []
        self.pending: List[Sequence] = []
        self.max_batch = max_batch_size

    def add_request(self, input_ids, max_gen=256):
        self.pending.append(Sequence(input_ids, 0, max_gen))

    def step(self, forward_fn):
        # 1. Pre-fill new sequences into batch if capacity exists
        while len(self.active) < self.max_batch and self.pending:
            seq = self.pending.pop(0)
            # Pre-fill: compute KV cache for prompt in one forward pass
            forward_fn(seq.tokens, prefill=True)
            self.active.append(seq)

        # 2. Decode step: one token forward pass for all active
        if self.active:
            next_tokens = forward_fn([s.tokens[-1:] for s in self.active], prefill=False)

        # 3. Update and evict finished sequences
        still_active = []
        for seq, next_tok in zip(self.active, next_tokens):
            seq.tokens.append(next_tok)
            seq.generated += 1
            if seq.generated >= seq.max_len or next_tok == 1:  # EOS=1
                seq.finished = True
                yield seq.tokens  # return completed sequence
            else:
                still_active.append(seq)
        self.active = still_active

# ponytail: real vLLM uses block-level page tables, not per-seq storage
# Upgrade path: add page table for KV cache to reduce fragmentation

sections.title: Request Routing & Autoscaling
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: A serving fleet has multiple GPU nodes, each running a model replica. The load balancer routes incoming requests to replicas. The autoscaler adds/removes replicas based on load. At Staff level, you must design routing and scaling to meet latency SLAs while minimizing idle GPU cost.

bar_chart Load Balancing Strategies: Round-robin: simplest but ignores load imbalance — a slow query can cascade latency. Least-connections: routes to the replica with fewest active requests — works well for uniform query sizes but fails when one query has 10× the tokens. Consistent hashing: hashes request ID to a replica — enables prefix cache reuse (same user/system prompt goes to same replica). For vLLM deployments, consistent hashing improves cache hit rate from 40% to 85%.

bar_chart Autoscaling Policies: CPU-based: scale up when queue depth exceeds threshold (e.g., >100 pending requests per replica). Latency-based: scale up when p99 latency exceeds target (e.g., p99 >500ms for 2 consecutive minutes). Request-based: scale on requests per second — predictable but can't handle sudden bursts. Production best practice: combine CPU-based (fast reaction) + latency-based (accurate) with 5-minute cooldown between scale events. Scale up aggressively (add 2 replicas at a time), scale down slowly (remove 1 at a time, 15-min cooldown).

track_changes MIG (Multi-Instance GPU): Slice an A100-80GB into up to 7× A100-10GB GPU instances. Each MIG slice has dedicated memory, cache, and compute — isolated and suitable for serving smaller models (7B-13B) at lower cost. A100-40GB: up to 3 MIG slices (each 10GB). H100: up to 7 MIG slices (each 10GB). MIG reduces per-query cost for small models by 3-7×.

sections.title: Latency Optimization — TTFT & TPOT
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: LLM serving latency has two components: TTFT (Time to First Token) — how long before the user sees the first word, and TPOT (Time Per Output Token) — the delay between each subsequent word. TTFT is dominated by prompt processing (compute-bound, proportional to prompt length). TPOT is dominated by memory bandwidth (reading the KV cache and weights for each token). Optimizing each requires different strategies.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: For Llama 3 70B on A100: TPOT = ~100ms/token (determined by 140GB weights / 2TB/s bandwidth). TTFT for a 2K prompt = ~200ms (attention flash at 120 TFLOPS). For a 128K prompt: TTFT = ~8s (quadratic attention is still heavy even with FlashAttention). Prompt prefill optimization: use FlashAttention-3, split long prompts across GPUs (sequence parallelism), and KV cache reuse (cache system prompts). TPOT optimization: INT4 quantization reduces weights from 140GB to 35GB → TPOT drops from 100ms to 25ms. Tensor parallelism across 8 GPUs: TPOT = 100ms / 8 = ~15ms with NVLink.

track_changes Prefill vs Decode Split Serving: Prefill GPUs (compute-optimized, e.g., A100) handle prompt processing. Decode GPUs (memory-optimized, e.g., A10G, cheaper) handle token generation. This specialization reduces cost by 30-50% because: prefill GPUs need high FLOPs (compute-bound) and decode GPUs need high memory bandwidth but can be cheaper (A10G at $1/hr vs A100 at $2/hr). The KV cache is transferred from prefill GPUs to decode GPUs via the model router.

search Staff Engineer Question: 'Design a serving system for a chat application with <100ms TTFT and <50ms TPOT.' Use 7B model (fast single-GPU) with speculative decoding (draft=100M model for TPOT). Pre-fill via FlashAttention-2 (fast TTFT). Deploy on A10G (cheaper). Route via consistent hashing (prefix cache reuse for system prompt). Use vLLM with continuous batching. Total: ~30ms TTFT + ~20ms TPOT comfortably under SLA.
sections.codeBlock:
# TTFT and TPOT optimization estimation

def estimate_ttft(prompt_tokens, compute_tflops=312, attn_tflops=120):
    """
    TTFT ≈ prompt attention time (dominates for long prompts).
    Complexity: O(n²) for attention, but FlashAttention makes it compute-bound.
    """
    flops_per_token = 8 * (prompt_tokens * 4096 * 4096)  # rough estimate
    total_flops = flops_per_token * prompt_tokens
    return total_flops / (attn_tflops * 1e12) * 1000  # ms

def estimate_tpot(memory_gb, hbm_bw_gbps=2000):
    """
    TPOT ≈ minimum time to read model weights from HBM.
    Each token needs the full model weights read from HBM.
    """
    return memory_gb / hbm_bw_gbps * 1000  # ms

models = [
    ('7B FP16', 14, 200, 312, 120),
    ('7B INT4', 3.5, 200, 312, 120),
    ('70B FP16', 140, 2000, 312, 120),
    ('70B INT4', 35, 2000, 312, 120),
]

for name, mem, prompt, compute, attn in models:
    ttft = estimate_ttft(prompt, compute, attn)
    tpot = estimate_tpot(mem, 2000)  # A100 HBM
    print(f'{name}: TTFT≈{ttft:.0f}ms, TPOT≈{tpot:.0f}ms')

# INT4 is the single biggest latency lever: 4× less memory to read

sections.title: Model Parallelism for Serving — Tensor vs Pipeline
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: When a model is too large for one GPU, you must split it across multiple GPUs. Tensor parallelism (TP): each layer's weights are sharded across GPUs. Every forward pass does an all-reduce to synchronize. Pipeline parallelism (PP): layers are split across GPUs (layers 0-N on GPU 0, N+1 to M on GPU 1). GPUs compute sequentially with micro-batch pipelining to hide the sequential bottleneck.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Llama 3 70B (140GB FP16): TP=8 (8× A100-80GB, each holding 17.5GB weights + KV cache). Llama 3 405B (810GB FP16): needs TP=8 × PP=2 (16 GPUs total). TP efficiency: TP=2 achieves ~95% of single-GPU throughput, TP=4 ~85%, TP=8 ~75%. The scaling overhead is from all-reduce communication — each transformer layer forward pass requires 2 all-reduce operations (one for attention, one for MLP). PP efficiency: With 4 micro-batches, PP=2 achieves ~90% utilization, PP=4 ~80%. The bubble overhead (idle time as pipeline drains) is the main inefficiency.

track_changes Communication Requirements: TP needs high-bandwidth interconnect: NVLink (600-900 GB/s) for intra-node TP. PP can use slower interconnects (InfiniBand 200-400 Gb/s, or even Ethernet) because communication is less frequent (only layer boundaries). For 8-GPU nodes: use TP=8 within the node (NVLink), PP across nodes (InfiniBand). This is the 8× TP per node + PP across nodes pattern used by major serving platforms.

search Staff Engineer Question: 'Our 70B model is deployed with TP=8 on 8× A100s. We want to reduce the GPU count to 4. Can we use TP=4?' Yes — but each GPU will hold 35GB of weights instead of 17.5GB, reducing available memory for KV cache. With TP=4, KV cache capacity drops from ~32K tokens/seq to ~16K tokens/seq. If your workload has 32K context, you must use TP=8. Alternative: use pipeline parallelism (PP=2 × TP=4) to split weights while keeping KV cache capacity.
sections.codeBlock:
# Tensor parallelism: all-reduce per layer (conceptual)
import torch, torch.distributed as dist

class TensorParallelLinear:
    """Linear layer sharded across GPUs — each GPU holds 1/tp columns."""
    def __init__(self, in_features, out_features, tp_rank, tp_size):
        # Shard output dimension: each GPU handles out_features/tp_size columns
        self.local_out = out_features // tp_size
        self.W = torch.randn(self.local_out, in_features)  # local shard
        self.tp_rank = tp_rank
        self.tp_size = tp_size

    def forward(self, x):
        # Local matmul on shard
        local_out = x @ self.W.T  # [B, local_out]
        # All-reduce across TP group to combine shards
        # ponytail: real impl uses all-reduce for A@W, or all-gather + reduce-scatter
        if self.tp_size > 1:
            dist.all_reduce(local_out, op=dist.ReduceOp.SUM)
        return local_out

# Pipeline parallelism: micro-batch scheduling
def pipeline_schedule(layers_device_map, micro_batches=4):
    """
    layers_device_map: {0: [0..10], 1: [11..20], ...}  # device -> layer ranges
    Micro-batches: split batch into M chunks, pipeline execution.
    """
    # F-then-B scheduling (1F1B): each micro-batch goes through
    # forward pass on all devices, then backward pass.
    schedule = []
    for mb in range(micro_batches):
        for device in layers_device_map:
            schedule.append(('F', device, mb))  # forward
    for device in reversed(layers_device_map):
        for mb in range(micro_batches):
            schedule.append(('B', device, mb))  # backward
    return schedule
    # ponytail: 1F1B reduces pipeline bubble to ~10% with 4 micro-batches

# Decision: TP when NVLink available, PP across nodes
# 70B: TP=8 (1 node, 8× A100-80G)
# 405B: TP=8 × PP=2 (2 nodes, 16× A100-80G)

sections.title: Cost Optimization — GPU Selection, Quantization, Spot
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: LLM serving costs are dominated by GPU rental. Minimizing cost per query requires choosing the right GPU, using quantization, leveraging spot instances, and maximizing GPU utilization through continuous batching and caching.

bar_chart GPU Selection: A100 ($2-3/hr on AWS), H100 ($4-6/hr), A10G ($1-1.5/hr), L40S ($1.5-2/hr), T4 ($0.5-1/hr). For inference: A10G has 24GB VRAM and 600GB/s bandwidth — sufficient for 13B models at $1/hr (50% cheaper than A100). For training: need H100/A100 for FLOPs. Rule: use A10G for models ≤13B, A100 for 13-70B, H100 for 70B+ or when latency SLA is tight. Quantized models (INT4) serve 4× more requests per GPU because 4× more model fits in VRAM, leaving more space for KV cache.

bar_chart Cost per Million Tokens: 7B on A100-80G = ~$0.56/M tokens (98 tokens/$). 70B on A100-80G = ~$3.70/M tokens (15 tokens/$). 70B INT4 on A100-80G = ~$2.22/M tokens (25 tokens/$). 7B on A10G = ~$0.40/M tokens (125 tokens/$). Caching saves 30-60% for chat applications — with 500-token system prompt cached: effective cost drops to $0.15/M tokens for 7B (system prompt is free after first cache fill).

track_changes Spot/Preemptible Instances: Spot instances are 60-70% cheaper than on-demand but can be preempted with 2-minute notice. Strategy: deploy replicas across a mix of spot (70%) and on-demand (30%) instances. If spot instances are reclaimed, the load balancer routes traffic to on-demand replicas while spot replacements spin up. For batch inference (no real-time requirement), use 100% spot.

search Staff Engineer Question: 'Reduce our inference cost from $50K/month to $20K/month without increasing latency.' Move from A100 ($2/hr) to A10G ($1/hr) for 7B models (50% savings). Quantize 70B models to INT4 (2× throughput → 50% fewer GPUs). Enable prefix caching (30-60% fewer tokens processed). Use spot instances for 70% of replicas (60% cheaper). Combined effect: ~70% reduction from $50K to ~$15K.
sections.codeBlock:
# Inference cost modeler
def monthly_cost(queries_per_day, input_tokens, output_tokens,
                 tok_per_sec, gpu_count, gpu_cost_hr, cache_hit_rate=0.0):
    tokens_per_query = input_tokens + output_tokens
    monthly_queries = queries_per_day * 30
    # Effective tokens: cache reduces input processing
    effective_tokens = monthly_queries * (
        (1 - cache_hit_rate) * input_tokens + output_tokens)
    seconds_needed = effective_tokens / tok_per_sec
    gpu_hours = seconds_needed / 3600
    # With N GPUs costing $X/hr
    cost = gpu_hours / gpu_count * gpu_cost_hr
    return cost

costs = [
    ('7B A100', 100, 5, 2.0, 0.0, monthly_cost(10e6, 1000, 200, 100, 5, 2.0)),
    ('7B A10G', 100, 1, 0.6, 0.0, monthly_cost(10e6, 1000, 200, 60, 8, 1.0)),
    ('7B A10G + cache', 100, 1, 0.6, 0.5, monthly_cost(10e6, 1000, 200, 60, 8, 1.0, 0.5)),
    ('70B A100', 15, 15, 2.0, 0.0, monthly_cost(10e6, 1000, 200, 15, 28, 2.0)),
    ('70B INT4 A100', 25, 15, 2.0, 0.0, monthly_cost(10e6, 1000, 200, 25, 15, 2.0)),
]

for name, _, _, _, _, cost in costs:
    print(f'{name}: ${cost/1e3:.1f}K/month')
# ponytail: assumes 80% GPU utilization — real util is 50-70%

sections.title: Production Metrics & Debugging
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: ML serving systems fail differently than traditional web services. The model can return 'correct' output but with unacceptable latency or cost. Staff engineers build monitoring across four dimensions: latency, throughput, quality, and resource utilization.

bar_chart Key Metrics to Track (p50/p99): TTFT — target: <100ms p50, <500ms p99. High TTFT indicates prompt processing bottleneck (long prompts, slow prefill). TPOT — target: <30ms p50, <100ms p99. High TPOT indicates memory bandwidth bottleneck (weighs too large for GPU, or KV cache thrashing). Throughput — target: GPU utilization 70-85%. Below 50%: underutilized (too many GPUs, or batch size too small). Above 90%: at risk of OOM or queue buildup. Queue depth — target: <50 msgs consistently. Spikes above 200: need to scale up. Error rate — target: <0.1% errors (timeout, OOM, invalid response). OOM errors indicate batch size or context length exceeds memory budget.

track_changes Debugging Slow Queries: Sample the top 1% of queries by total latency. Common causes: 1) Long prompt attention tail — some queries have 10× longer generation (e.g., complex chain-of-thought reasoning). Solution: per-query timeout, cap max_tokens per request. 2) KV cache eviction — if using prefix caching, the cache may be cold for new prefixes, causing full KV recomputation. 3) GPU memory fragmentation — even with PagedAttention, long-running sequences can fragment the block table. 4) Cross-node communication — slow inter-node links cause TP/PP stalls. Check NVLink errors, InfiniBand congestion.

track_changes Capacity Planning: Target 70% GPU utilization with 30% headroom for traffic spikes. Formula: GPUs = (peak_RPS × avg_gen_tokens) / (tok_per_sec_per_GPU × 0.7). Example: 1000 RPS, 200 tokens/response, 15 tok/s/GPU (70B) → (1000 × 200) / (15 × 0.7 × 3600) = ~5.3 → 6 GPUs. With headroom: 6 / 0.7 = ~9 GPUs. Always plan for 2× traffic during launches or seasonal peaks.

search Interview Angle: 'Our p99 latency went from 300ms to 2s after a deployment. What do you do?' 1) Check the diff: was the model changed (larger size, different quantization)? Was batching config changed? 2) Check GPU metrics: memory utilization near 100%? → KV cache issue. 3) Check the top-1% slowest queries: are they long generations? → cap max_tokens. 4) Check queue depth: is autoscaling slow to react? → reduce scale-up cooldown. 5) Rollback to previous working deployment while investigating.
sections.codeBlock:
# Production serving monitoring — conceptual dashboard
import time, statistics

class ServingMetrics:
    def __init__(self):
        self.ttft = []
        self.tpot = []
        self.queue_depth = 0
        self.errors = 0
        self.total_requests = 0

    def record_query(self, prompt_tokens, generated_tokens, start_time):
        self.total_requests += 1
        ttft = time.time() - start_time  # time to first token
        self.ttft.append(ttft)
        # TPOT is per-token latency reported by the engine

    def report(self):
        ttft_sorted = sorted(self.ttft)
        n = len(ttft_sorted)
        return {
            'ttft_p50': ttft_sorted[n // 2],
            'ttft_p99': ttft_sorted[int(n * 0.99)],
            'tpot_p50': None,  # populated by engine callback
            'throughput': self.total_requests / 300,  # qps over 5min window
            'error_rate': self.errors / max(self.total_requests, 1),
            'queue_depth': self.queue_depth,
        }

    def check_alerts(self, report):
        alerts = []
        if report['ttft_p99'] > 0.5:
            alerts.append(f"HIGH_TTFT: p99={report['ttft_p99']:.3f}s")
        if report['error_rate'] > 0.001:
            alerts.append(f"HIGH_ERRORS: rate={report['error_rate']:.4f}")
        if report['queue_depth'] > 200:
            alerts.append(f"QUEUE_BACKLOG: depth={report['queue_depth']}")
        return alerts

# Alert threshold: p95 TTFT > 500ms → auto-investigate
# Auto-slow-query-sampler: log top 1% slowest queries daily

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• vLLM: PagedAttention for LLM Serving (Kwon et al., 2023) — The core paper behind vLLM, introducing PagedAttention and continuous batching. 2-4× throughput improvement over prior art.
  • Effective Continuous Batching for LLM Serving — Iteration-level scheduling that adds/removes sequences on-the-fly. 5-20× over static batching.
  • TensorRT-LLM: Optimized LLM Inference on NVIDIA GPUs — NVIDIA's optimization toolkit: FP8/INT4 kernels, in-flight batching, multi-GPU parallelism.
  • Splitwise: LLM Inference on Two GPUs — Separates prefill (compute-bound) and decode (memory-bound) phases onto different GPU types. Reduces cost 30-50%.
  • Serving Llama 3 405B at Scale — Meta's serving architecture for 405B: TP=8 × PP=2 across 16 GPUs, FP8 quantization, prefix caching.

details: Serving Architecture Decision Flow: 1) Model size: ≤13B → single GPU (A10G cheaper); 13-70B → TP=4 to TP=8 (A100); >70B → TP + PP across nodes. 2) Quantization: INT4 for latency-sensitive (4× less memory bandwidth); FP8 for H100 maximum throughput. 3) Batching: vLLM continuous batching by default; disable only if workload has zero variability (unlikely). 4) Routing: consistent hashing for chat/assistants (prefix cache reuse); least-connections for batch inference. 5) Autoscaling: CPU-based (fast) + latency-based (accurate) hybrid. 6) Caching: enable prefix caching (30-60% token reduction for chat). 7) Cost: spot 70% + on-demand 30% for production; 100% spot for batch.
